text: The surgeon opens the peritoneum between the round ligament and the infundibulopelvic (IP) ligament to access and control the uterine arteries at their origin.
The surgeon uses an ultrasonic scalpel during the laparoscopic myomectomy, which is visible in the surgical field, to efficiently cut and coagulate tissue, aiming to reduce operative time.
In the retroperitoneal space, the surgeon identifies the internal iliac artery laterally and the uterine structures medially, using these landmarks to guide the procedure.
The surgeon opens the pararectal fossa between the uterus and the internal iliac artery by applying lateral traction, creating a visible space in the retroperitoneal area.
The surgeon opens the medial paramedical fossa, revealing the anatomical space between the uterus and surrounding structures.

The surgical field displays a space bordered medially by the bladder and laterally by the umbilical artery. The internal iliac artery, umbilical artery, and the origin of the uterine artery are clearly visible.
The surgeon places a clip on the origin of the right uterine artery, visibly reducing blood flow to the uterus.
The surgeon performs the same procedure on the opposite side, opening the space between the uterus and the internal iliac artery, known as the pararectal fossa, using lateral traction.
The surgeon applies traction to the left infundibulopelvic (IP) ligament, visually manipulating it within the surgical field.

The surgeon uses an ultrasonic device to remove small attachments from the sacrum, allowing deeper access into the retroperitoneal space. The internal iliac artery is clearly visible laterally within this space.

The surgeon dissects the space between the ureter medially and the internal iliac artery laterally, using left iliac forceps to develop the retroperitoneal space. The assistant tracks the IP ligament medially to enhance visibility.

The surgeon tracks the umbilical artery, the anterior trunk of the internal iliac artery, within the lateral paravesical fossa. The uterine artery, the medial trunk of the internal iliac artery, is also visible, with clear anatomical structures observed.
The surgeon visually identifies the uterine artery and umbilical artery within the paramedical fossa, with the ureter clearly visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon medializes the ureter and places a clip on the origin of the left uterine artery, visible within the retroperitoneal space.
The surgeon decreases uterine vascularization using a specific maneuver, preparing the surgical field for a safe laparoscopic myomectomy.
The surgeon uses a Harmonic ACE device in cutting mode to incise the myometrial serosa, beginning the process of opening the uterus.

The surgeon uses the harmonic ACE device to track and incise the serosa longitudinally, aiming to reduce the risk of intraoperative bleeding and minimize tissue burning. The surgical field is clearly visible.

The surgeon uses 10mm forceps to track the myoma, aiming to decrease perioperative bleeding and improve visibility of the dissecting plane. The harmonic ACE device is used to cut the myometrial capsular tissue.
The surgeon achieves perfect visual control while opening the uterus over a seven-centimeter area to access a ten-centimeter myoma.
The surgeon uses a ten millimeter forceps, positioning it laterally to enhance retraction and improve visibility of the dissection area during the laparoscopic myomectomy.

The surgeon applies traction to achieve clear visualization of the dissection area. The procedure appears bloodless following normal uterine control, with the forceps being repositioned as needed to cut tissue directly in contact.

The surgeon continues the dissection of the fibroid using the harmonic ACE device, carefully separating it from the uterine myometrium. The harmonic ACE is visible as it cuts through the tissue, minimizing bleeding and enhancing visual clarity.
The surgeon carefully dissects around the fibroid, aiming to spare the endometrial cavity while maintaining visual control during the myomectomy procedure.
The surgeon observes the myoma, noting its significant impact on the shape and size of the endometrial cavity, which is visibly distorted by the presence of the fibroid.

The surgeon cuts the tissue directly in contact with the myoma, avoiding the residual endometrial material. The myomectomy is nearly complete, with minimal bleeding observed, eliminating the need for additional hemostasis using bipolar NLV.
The surgeon controls the intracavitary myoma and identifies one or two endometrial polyps. The polyps are prepared for removal using a Harmonic scalpel.
The surgeon exposes the endometrial cavity, revealing its structure and spatial relationship with the myoma.
The surgeon begins hemostasis, applying techniques to control bleeding in the surgical field after the myomectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses a VigFill needle with a 14mm diameter to perform the Entracapoeia knotting technique, placing the stitch 1.5 centimeters outside the uterine scar.
The surgeon invaginates the healthy uterine tissue into the myoma bed to enhance hemostasis and reduce the risk of post-operative complications.
The surgeon ties a personal knot, effectively invaginating the healthy myometrium into the myoma bed, enhancing hemostasis.
The surgeon retracts the stitches cranially, adjusting their position to aid in hemostasis within the myoma bed.
The assistants invaginate the healthy myometrial tissue into the myoma bed, ensuring proper placement within the surgical site.

The surgeon uses an intra-corporeal knotting technique to lock the knot, ensuring a secure closure within the myoma bed. The knot is positioned upward, demonstrating precise control over the suture placement.
The surgeon places a suture one and a half centimeters outside the uterine scar using a fourteen millimeter needle, mirroring the technique on the opposite side.
The surgeon uses a double flat knot technique to invaginate the healthy myometrium into the myoma bed, visibly reducing the potential for post-operative synechiae and bowel adhesions.

The surgeon uses the intracorporeal knotting technique, employing the crocodile method, while the assistants invaginate the tissue inside the myoma bed. The tissue is retracted cranially during the procedure.
The surgeon completes the suture by tying the last knot above the previous one, ensuring both edges of the suture are neatly aligned.
The surgeon carefully avoids including the right and left tubal cords in the stitch while tying three or four knots to achieve uterine reconstruction and hemostasis, without using bipolar cautery.
The surgeon inserts a 15mm morcellator into the left iliac operative trocar to perform morcellation of the myoma, with the device set at 700 revolutions per minute for efficient tissue removal.

The surgeon completes the morcellation of the myoma in less than three minutes using a morcellator. The abdominal cavity is then rinsed to visually inspect the uterine scar for any abnormalities or bleeding.
The surgeon demonstrates control of both uterine arteries at their origin, with the pelvis visibly free of blood. The uterine scar appears well-constructed, indicating successful hemostasis.
